claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances and your children.

Let me start with the basics:

**How many children are you responsible for, and what are their names and ages?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hello! I'm looking to claim Child Benefit and want to check if I'm eligible.

I have one child - Alex, who is 17 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since Alex is 17 years old, I need to ask about their current education or training situation, as this affects eligibility.

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** For example, are they attending school, college, university, or an apprenticeship?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex recently left school, but they've just signed up with the careers service to figure out what they want to do next.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful to know. Since Alex has registered with a government-sponsored careers service after leaving education, they may still be eligible for Child Benefit for a limited period.

Before I can give you a final answer, I need to confirm one more important detail:

**When did Alex leave school and register with the careers service?** Specifically, I need to know if this happened within the last 20 weeks.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, it's been within the last 20 weeks. They left school and registered with the careers service recently, so they're still in that extension period.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! I now have all the information I need to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit.

Based on your circumstances:

- You have one child, Alex, who is 17 years old
- Alex recently left school and has registered with a government-sponsored careers service
- This registration occurred within the last 20 weeks

According to the Child Benefit rules, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with a government-sponsored careers service. Since Alex meets these criteria and is still within that 20-week extension period, you are eligible.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Great news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Alex qualifies for the special 20-week extension period that applies to 16 and 17 year olds who leave education or training and register with a government-sponsored careers service. Since Alex has done this within the last 20 weeks, you can claim Child Benefit for them during this period.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and recently left approved education. They have registered with a government-sponsored careers service within the last 20 weeks. According to Child Benefit rules, you can claim Child Benefit for a 16 or 17 year old for 20 weeks if they leave education or training and register with a government-sponsored careers service. Alex meets all these criteria and is still within the 20-week extension period, making them eligible.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. Although Alex is 17 and no longer in full-time education, they qualify for the 20-week extension period because they have registered with a government-sponsored careers service after leaving school, and this registration occurred within the qualifying timeframe.">

According to Child Benefit rules, you can claim Child Benefit for a 16 or 17 year old for 20 weeks if they leave education or training and register with a government-sponsored careers service. Alex meets all these criteria and is still within the 20-week extension period, making them eligible.

Although Alex is 17 and no longer in full-time education, they qualify for the 20-week extension period because they have registered with a government-sponsored careers service after leaving school, and this registration occurred within the qualifying timeframe.">
